coredata.dc:title: A carbon-dot fluorescence capillary sensor for the determination of chemical oxygen demand
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Rong
Li, Yong-Sheng
Luo, Ya-Xiong
Zhang, Xu-Yan
Wen, Rong
Gao, Xiu-Feng
coredata.dc:description: The development of a highly sensitive, reusable, and microportable sensor for determining the chemical oxygen demand (COD) is very important for performing in situ measurements. A reusable carbon-dot fluorescence capillary sensor is reported here. Citric acid/urea-carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment and immobilized on the inner surface of a capillary using sol–gel technology. The sensor were used in conjunction with a recirculating-flow system to establish a set of methods for the indirect determination of COD in water by reaction with KMnO4. The carbon dots reduce KMnO4 to produce MnO2, thereby quenching the fluorescence of the sensor through the adsorption/inner filter effect. We harnessed this mechanism to successfully determine the CODMn (permanganate index) in the range of 0.2–40 mg/L (ΔF = -48.32c + 678.25, r = 0.9996, 0.2–12 mg/L; ΔF = -16.31c + 792.61, r = 0.9998, 8–40 mg/L). The sensor was subsequently regenerated by using ascorbic acid to reduce the carbon dots and remove the MnO2 adsorbed on the inner surface. The sensor could be reused at least 20 times with a relative standard deviation of less than 0.59 %. The proposed method was applied to quantify the COD in potable, waste and natural water. Combining the sensor with the automated flow system is a potential method for in situ real-time monitoring of COD in water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence capillary sensor
Chemical oxygen demand
Reirculating-flow system
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of CA/urea-CDs and the CD-FCS
Feasibility study of COD measurement by the CD-FCS
Establishment of the COD determination method
Optimization of the conditions for the digestion of water samples
Conditional optimization for COD determination by the CD-FCS
Effect of the Cl- on the COD determination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Infrared spectra (a); Fluorescence spectrum (b); TEM image (c); Particle size (d).
Optimization of oxidants (a); Acid stability of CD-FCS (b); Feasibility of measuring acid KMnO4 (c).

Overlap diagram of excitation, emission spectra of CD-FCS and absorption spectra of KMnO4 (a); Effect of different reagents on fluorescence recovery of CD-FCS (b); XPS spectrum (Mn 2p) of CD-FCS after reaction with KMnO4 (c); Sensing mechanism (d).
Linear curve (a) and recording curve of reproducibility (b) of COD determination with CD-FCS.

head.ce:title: A carbon-dot fluorescence capillary sensor for the determination of chemical oxygen demand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: West China School of Basic Medical Sciences & Forensic Medicine, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: West China School of Basic Medical Sciences & Forensic Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: West China School of Basic Medical Sciences & Forensic Medicine, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of a highly sensitive, reusable, and microportable sensor for determining the chemical oxygen demand (COD) is very important for performing in situ measurements. A reusable carbon-dot fluorescence capillary sensor is reported here. Citric acid/urea-carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment and immobilized on the inner surface of a capillary using sol–gel technology. The sensor were used in conjunction with a recirculating-flow system to establish a set of methods for the indirect determination of COD in water by reaction with KMnO4. The carbon dots reduce KMnO4 to produce MnO2, thereby quenching the fluorescence of the sensor through the adsorption/inner filter effect. We harnessed this mechanism to successfully determine the CODMn (permanganate index) in the range of 0.2–40 mg/L (ΔF = -48.32c + 678.25, r = 0.9996, 0.2–12 mg/L; ΔF = -16.31c + 792.61, r = 0.9998, 8–40 mg/L). The sensor was subsequently regenerated by using ascorbic acid to reduce the carbon dots and remove the MnO2 adsorbed on the inner surface. The sensor could be reused at least 20 times with a relative standard deviation of less than 0.59 %. The proposed method was applied to quantify the COD in potable, waste and natural water. Combining the sensor with the automated flow system is a potential method for in situ real-time monitoring of COD in water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid industrial development and rising global populations have made water pollution an urgent problem for mankind, [1], which restricts economic development and endangers human life and health. Testing water quality is the first step in water pollution control, and the chemical oxygen demand (COD) is one of the important indicators of water quality [2]. The COD is defined as the quantity of oxygen consumed during the oxidation of organic compounds in water and represents the content of these compounds in water [3]. The decomposition of an organic compound in water by microorganisms consumes oxygen dissolved in water. Excessive oxygen consumption affects the life of other aquatic organisms. Once the dissolved oxygen in the water is exhausted, organic compounds decompose anaerobically, producing odorous gases, such as hydrogen sulfide and mercaptan, that further deteriorate the water quality. The more severe organic pollution in water is, the higher the COD is. Therefore, it is vital to monitor the COD in water [4].

The main methods currently used for COD determination include titration, spectrophotometry, chemiluminescence, electrochemistry, ultraviolet absorption spectrometry and three-dimensional fluorescence spectrometry. UV absorption spectrometry and three-dimensional fluorescence spectrometry are simple and rapid, do not require chemical reagents or digestion steps and do not produce secondary pollution [5]. The characteristic absorption of different organic compounds in the range of 200–700 nm [6,7] and the fluorescence intensity of organic compounds determined using different excitation and emission wavelengths [8,9] were used to quantify the COD in water. However, as different chemical substances have mutual interference and absorbance overlap, the COD cannot be quantified using a single absorbance or fluorescence intensity [10]. Thus, it is necessary to investigate the use of suitable data analysis methods [7], such as parallel factor analysis [11], principal component analysis [12], partial least squares regression [13,14] and regression models [15], for COD determination.

Electrochemical techniques quantify COD through the voltage and current generated by electron transfer. The basic principle of these methods is that the application of a constant voltage to the working electrode generates hydroxyl radicals (·OH) by electrolysis that oxidize organic compounds in water, accompanied by electron transfer [16]. Lee et al. [17] first used Cu as a working electrode to determine the COD in the range of 1–10 g/L. However, as Cu exhibits a low peroxide potential, Ai et al. [18] measured the COD using a nano PbO2 electrode, which has a higher potential than that of a Cu electrode. A low detection limit of 2.5 mg/L with a 5–3000 mg/L linear range was achieved. The lack of widespread use of PbO2 electrodes can be attributed to the risk of release of toxic heavy metals (Pb) [19]. Yu et al. [20] used boron-doped diamond (BDD), which has an extensive electrocatalytic working potential window [21] and corrosion resistance, to detect the COD in the range of 20–9000 mg/L with a low detection limit of 7.5 mg/L. Using BBD as an electrode broadens the COD detection range [21], but BBD has a complex fabrication process and high preparation cost. All the aforementioned electrodes are still insufficiently reliable for practical use mainly due to their inability to oxidize a wide spectrum of organic compounds in water [2]. Lee et al. [22] made the first attempts to fix TiO2 particles with photocatalytic activity on a working electrode, which promoted the generation of photogenerated holes [23] and ·OH [24] on the surface of TiO2 and the electrode and enhanced the oxidation degree of organic matter, with a COD detection limit of 0.118 mg/L. This synergistic photoelectric catalysis method has a low detection limit [25]. However, TiO2 electrodes have a fatal drawback in practice, the poor utilization of visible light. Only UV light at wavelengths shorter than 390 nm can be absorbed by pure TiO2. Wang et al. [26] used a Cu2O-loaded TiO2 nanotube array electrode, where Cu2O extended the spectral absorption to 652 nm, resulting in the determination of 20–300 mg/L COD under visible light irradiation, with a detection limit of 15 mg/L. The photocatalytic method has the advantages of green environmental protection and strong oxidation ability but has poor reproducibility [27] for COD measurement because of the facile recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

The potassium dichromate method (CODCr) [28] and permanganate index method (CODMn) [29] are standard titration methods used for COD determination. The CODCr method consists of the following steps: an oxidant (Cr2O7 2-), a catalyst (Ag+) and H2SO4 are added to an aqueous sample that is then heated for 2 h, and the COD in the water is determined by titrating the residual Cr2O7 2- with a Fe2+/1,10-phenanthroline solution, where the color change of the reaction solution from yellow to blue-green to red brown is the end point. The CODMn method consists of the following steps: acidic KMnO4 is used to oxidize aquatic organic compounds, C2O4 2- is added to the reaction mixture to reduce the remaining MnO4 -, and the excess C2O4 2- is finally titrated with KMnO4. The COD is determined by the KMnO4 consumed to change the color of the reaction solution (colorless → pink). However, as the accuracy of titration is easily affected by human factors, a spectrophotometric method for detecting COD has been established. Quantification of COD is based on the characteristic absorption of the oxidants Cr2O7 2- (425 nm) [30], KMnO4 (525 nm) [31], Ce4+ (320 nm) [32] or the reduction product Cr3+ (620 nm) [33]. Korenaga et al. [34] first proposed FIA spectrophotometry to determine the COD in 1980, achieving a detection limit of 10 mg/L and further reducing human operation error. The chemiluminescence (CL) method is more sensitive than spectrophotometry and has therefore been widely studied for quantifying the COD in water [35–36]. Hu et al. [37] used potassium dichromate as an oxidant, where the reaction product Cr3+ catalyzes the CL system of 3-aminophthalimide (luminol)–H2O2, enabling the indirect quantification of the COD in water with a detection limit of 2.1 mg/L. Yao et al. [38] used KMnO4 to oxidize organic compounds in water, where the residual KMnO4 and glutaraldehyde formed a CL system to realize the determination of 0.16–19.24 mg/L COD with a detection limit of 0.1 mg/L. Cristina et al. [39] constructed a luminol/CdTe CL system, in which CdTe nanocrystals were subjected to UV irradiation to generate ·OH as an oxidant, thereby realizing COD determination in the range of 1–35 mg/L. The lack of widespread application of the CL technique can be attributed to the use of toxic and expensive CL reagents: H2O2, glutaraldehyde, and CdTe nanomaterials [40]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a novel method with high sensitivity, a low cost, simple operation and environmental friendliness.

In fluorescence capillary analysis (FCA), trace-level analysis of microvolume samples is realized by replacing the detection cell used in the traditional fluorescence method with a capillary [41,42]. The cost of fluorescence analysis and waste pollution from analytical chemistry are thus significantly reduced. The FCA method is based on many mature methods used in traditional fluorescence spectroscopy, such as direct determination, chemical conversion, fluorescence quenching, catalytic fluorescence kinetics, photosensitized luminescence and time-resolved fluorescence analysis; however, immobilized fluorescence capillary analysis (immobilized-FCA) [43,44] has higher sensitivity and selectivity. The use of a capillary as a carrier to immobilize fluorescent reagents in the immobilized-FCA method significantly reduces the consumption of fluorescent reagents, provides green environmental protection, and enables the miniaturization and portability of laboratory fluorophotometers [45]. Therefore, a carbon-dot fluorescence capillary sensor (CD-FCS) for reproducible determination of CODMn in water was prepared in this study. Sol-gel technology [46] was used to immobilize carbon dots with reversible redox performance [47] on the inner surface of a capillary. A redox reaction was performed using KMnO4-ASA to provide input signals, and fluorescence changes were used as readout signals. Combined with a circulating flow system [48], the CD-FCS can respond highly sensitively to residual KMnO4 after organic compounds have been oxidized through water enrichment with KMnO4, thereby saving oxidants and providing a method for COD detection in rivers, drinking water and polluted wastewater with high sensitivity, portability, environmental friendliness, a low cost and simple operation.

The main instruments used in this study included RF-5301-PC fluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan); ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai Aoyi, China); X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ThermoFisher, USA); Nicolet 6700 infrared spectrometer (FEI Company, USA); Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI Company, USA); DZF-6020 oven with a constant temperature and vacuum drying function (Chengdu Kelong, China); 25-mL PTFE high-pressure reactor (Xian Mojina, China); AUW electronic balance (accuracy: 0.0001 g; Shimadzu, Japan); K30 digital dry baths (temperature control range: 0–100 °C; Hangzhou Aosheng, China); Dw10-1 peristaltic pump (Baoding LeadFluid, China); glass capillary (45 mm × 0.90 mm, West China Medical University Glass Instrument Factory, China); and capillary precision-positioning holder (13 mm × 13 mm × 43 mm, homemade).

The main reagents used in this study included CA, urea, H2SO4, KMnO4, potassium hydrogen phthalate (KHP), ascorbic acid (ASA), and γ-glycidyl ether hydroxypropyl trimethoxysilane (KH560). These reagents were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. and were analytically pure. The water used for the experiments was pure (conductivity: ∼ 0.5 μS cm−1).

Equal volumes of 2 mol/L CA and 6 mol/L urea were poured into a PTFE-lined stainless-steel autoclave and reacted for 3 h at 180℃ to obtain a crude suspension product [47]. The suspension was placed in a dialysis bag with a MW of 500, which was then placed in a 1000-mL beaker filled with ultrapure water, and the water was changed every 4 h until no fluorescence was detected in the liquid outside the dialysis bag. The liquid was vacuum-dried at 40 °C, to yield solid powdered CA/urea-CDs.

Immobilized capillary fluorescence sensors were fabricated by enzyme immobilization in a capillary [43] and the sol–gel method [46]. First, the capillary was pretreated. The glass capillary was boiled in a 1 M NaOH solution for 1 h, washed with water and alcohol, and dried at 40 °C for standby. Second, the pretreated capillary tube was used to suck a mixed solution (2 mL 12.5 g/L CA/urea-CDs + 2 mL KH560 + 0.1 mL 2 mol/L H2SO4) and was then rotated continuously at 40 °C for 30 h in the dark. Third, the liquid in the capillary tube was removed with absorbent paper and cured at 40 °C for 72 h. Finally, the capillary was removed from the solution, washed with ultrapure water several times to remove the floating layer, and dried of internal and external moisture at 40 °C to yield the CD-FCS, which was stored at room temperature.

Two milliliters of a sample + 1 mL of 80 mg/L KMnO4 + 1 mL of 0.02 mol/L H2SO4 were thoroughly mixed and placed in a 5 mL centrifuge tube, which was covered tightly and placed in a 100 °C thermostat for 20 min of digestion. The digested samples were immediately quenched in cold water to stop the reaction, and a 0.22-μm U. M needle filter was used to filter out the MnO2 produced during the reaction. The digested solution was thus obtained.

The recirculating-flow fluorescence capillary system (RF-FCS) designed in this study is shown in Fig. 1 . This system is composed of a peristaltic pump, a fluorophotometer, a capillary fluorescence flow detection cell, a display, a reagent/sample container and a pipeline. The system is operated as follows: first, Valve A is switched to No. 1 “Clean” and Valve B is switched to No. 4 “Waste”; the pump is turned on; the pipeline is cleaned with sulfuric acid, and the fluorescence (F0) of the reagent blank is measured (λe/m = 360/440 nm). Then, both Valves A and B are switched to No. 3 “Injection”, and the action of the peristaltic pump enables the reaction of the digested solution with the CD-FCS in the circulation flow path for 10 min, and the quenched fluorescence (F) is determined. Finally, Valve A is switched to No. 2 “Regeneration”, and Valve B is switched to No. 4 “Waste”. The action of the pump enables the CD-FCS to react with ASA to restore the sensor fluorescence and thereby regenerate the sensor. The measurement of one sample is thus completed. The sample is replaced, and the next sample is measured (the sample volume is fixed). The COD is quantified by the difference in fluorescence measurements (ΔF = F0 – F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CA/urea-CDs and the CD-FCS

ce:section.ce:para: Infrared spectroscopy was carried out to analyze the surface functional groups of the CA/urea-CDs and CD-FCS, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 a. In the infrared spectra of the CA/urea-CDs, the peaks at wavenumbers of 3100, 1750, 1568, and 1331 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of CH, CO, CC, and CN, respectively. The peak at 3422 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH (carboxyl, associating) and the NH of the amide group. The peak at 1394 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of NH. These results showed that the main functional groups on the surface of the CA/urea-CDs are OH, COOH, CC and NH2. This evidence demonstrated that the CA/urea-CDs were successfully prepared [47]. In the CD-FCS spectrum, the peaks at 2900, 1750, and 1500 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CH, CO and CC, respectively, and the peaks in the range of 1250–1000 cm−1 were assigned to Si–O–Si bending–stretching vibrations [49]. The absorption peaks for OH and NH in the range of 3500–3300 cm−1 were almost indiscernible. Thus, the IR results confirmed that the CD-FCS surface was mainly composed of CO and CC and SiOSi. Comparing the infrared spectra of the products before and after immobilization showed that OH and NH were replaced during the immobilization process: the absorption peaks of CH, CC and CO in the original CD-FCS spectrum were retained in the spectrum of the immobilized sample, and a new absorption peak of Si–O–Si appeared in the spectrum of the immobilized sample. These results were attributed to the hydrolysis of KH560 during the immobilization process and successful silylation, indicating the successful preparation of the CD-FCS.

Figs. 2b and 4a show the absorption and emission spectra that were recorded to explore the fluorescence characteristics of the CA/urea-CDs and CD-FCS. The maximum emission wavelength of the CA/urea-CDs was 440 nm, and the maximum excitation wavelength was 360 nm. The peak at 360 nm was attributed to the n–π* (CO) transition of the surface groups with lone pair electrons. The inset in the upper right corner of Fig. 2b consists of photographs of the CDs exposed to natural and ultraviolet light. The maximum emission and excitation wavelengths of the CD-FCS were not different from those of the carbon dots, indicating that immobilization did not change the fluorescence characteristics of the CDs. The inset in Fig. 4a is a photograph of the sensor exposed to natural and ultraviolet light.

The morphology of the CA/urea-CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy, and the results are shown in Fig. 2c. The CDs are spherical and uniformly dispersed in aqueous solution, which indicates that the CDs prepared under hydrothermal conditions have good dispersion. The particle size distribution presented in Fig. 2d shows that the particle size of the CDs ranges between 10 and 40 nm and the average particle size is approximately 24 nm, indicating a normal distribution overall.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility study of COD measurement by the CD-FCS
ce:section.ce:para: KMnO4, K2Cr2O7 and H2O2 are commonly used as oxidants for COD determination. MnO4 -, Cr2O7 2- and H2O2 were separately reacted with the CD-FCS in the recirculation-flow system, the sensor was cleaned with water and ASA, and the fluorescence kinetics curve was determined (λe/m = 360/440 nm). Fig. 3 a shows that the addition of H2O2 has no effect on the fluorescence of the CD-FCS; the fluorescence quenching of the CDs by Cr2O7 2- is only an inner filter effect (IFE), and the fluorescence of the CD-FCS can be recovered by washing with water, whereas fluorescence quenching by MnO4 - cannot be recovered by water washing and can only be recovered by washing with ASA. Therefore, the CD-FCS have high selectivity and sensitivity for MnO4 - and are suitable for indirect determination of the COD in water by KMnO4. To prevent interference from other ions, solutions of various ions were prepared at 100 μmol/L (Fe3+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Sn2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Co2+, NH4 +, Li+, SCN-, ClO4 -, I-, BrO3 -, NO2 −, F-, MnO4 -, Cl-, NO3 −, H2PO4 -, Cr2O7 2-, S2-, SO4 2-, SiO3 2-, and CO3 2−). The CD-FCS was used to suck up the solutions containing different ions, and the fluorescence was recorded before and after a 20-min reaction period (Fig. S5). The fluorescence of the CD-FCS could only be strongly quenched by MnO4 - and Cr2O7 2- and was almost unaffected by the other ions. This result further proves that the CD-FCS has good selectivity for KMnO4.

KMnO4 can oxidize organic matter in water only under acidic conditions, but the feasibility of determining acidic KMnO4 by the CD-FCS has not been investigated. Thus, the stability of the CD-FCS in acid was investigated in this study by generating a fluorescence kinetic curve (Fig. 3b). The results showed that in the pH range of 1–7, reacting H2SO4 with the CD-FCS enhanced the fluorescence, which remained at a stable value. The CD-FCS was washed, and the fluorescence returned to the initial value. For a pH < 1, the fluorescence of the sensor became unstable and was not recovered after cleaning. Thus, it can be concluded that the sensor is stable in acid with pHs of 1–7. Next, the feasibility of determining acidic KMnO4 by the CD-FCS was explored (Fig. 3c). In the pH range of 3–7, the quenched fluorescence (ΔF) increases with the solution acidity. For solution pHs below 3, ΔF decreases with increasing acidity. This behavior depends strongly on the sensing mechanism of KMnO4. Although the fluorescence of the sensors is quenched by KMnO4 with different acidic pHs to different degrees, the fluorescence recovery of the sensor demonstrates the feasibility of determining acidic KMnO4.

It is worth exploring the sensing mechanism that enables the sensor fluorescence to be quenched through reaction with KMnO4. First, the influence of the KMnO4 reactant on the fluorescence quenching of the sensor was investigated. We superimposed the UV visible absorption peak of KMnO4 on the excitation and absorption peaks of the CD-FCS (Fig. 4a). KMnO4 was found to have absorption at the optimal excitation of 360 nm but almost no absorption at the optimal emission of 450 nm, which indicates that KMnO4 may have an inner filter effect (IFE) on the excitation light but has no effect on the emission light. The IFE of KMnO4 was investigated as follows: KMnO4 was allowed to completely react with the CD-FCS in the recirculating-flow system, the sensor was cleaned with pure water, the fluorescence kinetic curve was obtained for the entire process, and the change in the sensor fluorescence was determined (Fig. 4 b). The results showed that after the sensor reacted with KMnO4, only a small part of the fluorescence could be recovered by water washing, which provides sufficient evidence that the IFE caused by KMnO4 is a small effect.

Next, the fluorescence quenching was correlated with the reaction products. The reaction between KMnO4 and CDs can produce Mn2+ or MnO2. KMnO4 was allowed to completely react with the CD-FCS in the circulation system, and ethylenediaminetetraace -tic acid (EDTA), ASA, NaBH4 and sodium ascorbate (C6H7O6Na) were then separately used to regenerate the sensor. The fluorescence kinetic curve for the entire process was determined and shown in Fig. 4b. EDTA can coordinate with Mn2+ to eliminate the interference of Mn2+ on the fluorescence of the sensor but EDTA does not restore the sensor fluorescence, which provides sufficient evidence that Mn2+ does not play a role in the quenching of the fluorescence of immobilized CDs by KMnO4. MnO2 is brown and easily adsorbed on solid surfaces. After the reaction with KMnO4, the capillary turned brown, indicating that MnO2 adsorption may have occurred [50,51]. It was speculated that MnO2 could be stripped from the inner surface of a sensor by the addition of a reducing agent: thus, the commonly available, nontoxic and harmless reducing agent ASA was added to the CD-FCS. The light green fluorescence of the sensor was completely restored after adding ASA, verifying the speculation. To confirm that the substance adsorbed on the sensor was MnO2, the sensor was allowed to completely react with KMnO4, the inner surface of the CD-FCS was scraped off, and the valence state of Mn on the sensor surface was determined using XPS. Fig. 4c shows the XPS spectra of Mn 2p could be fitted with two peaks at 641.7 eV and 653.4 eV, which correspond to Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2, respectively. The binding energy (641.7 eV) of Mn 2p3/2 is basically consistent with the binding energy (641.8 eV) of Mn 2p3/2 in the standard database. The splitting energy of Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 is 11.7 eV, which is consistent with a previous report [52] and confirms that the product adsorbed on the sensor was MnO2.

To further investigate the influence of the reducing agent on the adsorption /IFE of MnO2 removal, NaBH4 and C6H7O6Na were selected as reductants to regenerate the sensor. The use of NaBH4 recovered most but not all the sensor fluorescence. Thus, NaBH4 was directly reacted with the sensor to investigate why the sensor fluorescence could not be completely restored. This behavior was found to result from NaBH4 reducing the fluorescence of the CD-FCS (Fig. S1). C6H7O6Na is a stronger reductant that ASA, but the kinetic curve shows that C6H7O6Na has a considerably lower fluorescence recovery rate than ASA. To exclude the interference of pH, the pH of C6H7O6Na was adjusted to 3 using sulfuric acid. The fluorescence recovery rate increased immediately, indicating that ASA restores the fluorescence of the CD-FCS due to the synergistic effect of reduction and acidity.

These results are summarized in Fig. 4d, which shows the sensing mechanism based on using KMnO4 and the CD-FCS: KMnO4 reacts with the CDs and is reduced to MnO2, which adsorbs very easily on the inner surface of the capillary to form a mask (brown layer), producing the IFE and quenching the fluorescence of the CD-FCS. When ASA enters the flow system, MnO2 is further reduced to Mn2+, which is removed with the liquid flow, and ASA can reduce the ox-CDs produced by oxidation with KMnO4 to re-CDs, thus regenerating the CD-FCS. This mechanism proves that the CD-FCS has the characteristics of redox-switching and can react reversibly with KMnO4/ASA. The quenched and recovered fluorescence of the sensor remained completely consistent over more than 20 repeated determinations (Fig. 5 b), indicating that the CD-FCS has excellent stability.

This mechanism also explains the relationship between pH and ΔF shown in Fig. 3c. When the CD-FCS reacts with KMnO4 at different pHs ranging from 7 to 3, the quenched fluorescence increases with the solution acidity, indicating that increasing the solution acidity moves the reaction to the right and increases the quantity of manganese dioxide, such that the quenched fluorescence of the sensor also increases. As the solution acidity is increased from pH 3 to pH 1, ΔF decreases continuously. This behavior results from an enhancement of the oxidation capacity of KMnO4 under highly acidic conditions and the generation of manganese ions, resulting in a decrease in the quantity of manganese dioxide and a decrease in the quenched fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the COD determination method
ce:section.ce:para: The abovementioned experimental results prove that the sensing mechanism of CDs-FCS and KMnO4 is adsorption of the MnO2 product/IFE. The enrichment effect of the circulating flow process enabled highly sensitive repeated determination of KMnO4. KMnO4 was used to oxidize organic compound in a water sample, and the residual KMnO4 was then quantified by the quenched fluorescence of CDs-FCS to indirectly quantify the COD in the water. A novel method for COD determination in water was thus effectively established.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the conditions for the digestion of water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Acidic KMnO4 can oxidize organic compounds in water at high temperature. To explore the influence of various factors on water-sample digestion, a KHP standard solution was used as a test sample to optimize the pH, temperature, reaction time and concentration of potassium permanganate used for the digestion process, thereby achieving water-sample digestion with low reagent consumption under the optimal conditions.

In the previous section, it was concluded that the CD-FCS emitted stable fluorescence in the range of pH = 1–7; thus, the pH of the digestion process for the water sample should be maintained in this range. To further optimize and determine the reaction acidity, a KHP standard solution (0–50 mg/L) was added to a 20 mg/L KMnO4 solution with pH = 1–7 and digested in a 100℃ thermostat for 25 min. The solution was immersed in cold water to cool to room temperature, and the MnO2 produced during the reaction process was removed by passing the solution through a 0.22-μm U.M needle filter. Then, the absorbance of the solution was measured at 525 nm. Fig. S3a shows the ΔA-COD curves obtained under different pHs. Within the pH range of 1–7, the ΔA-COD curve is linear only at pH 1 and pH 2. For pH = 1–2, KMnO4 mainly reacts with the KHP in the water to generate Mn2+, resulting in few byproducts and a linear curve. At pH = 3–6, the oxidation capacity of KMnO4 is not sufficiently strong because of the low solution acidity. The product of the reaction with KHP is a mixture of Mn2+ and MnO2, such that the curve is no longer linear in the concentration. At pH = 7, KHP does not react completely with the reducing substances in the water, such that ΔA is close to 0. The slope of the linear curve obtained at pH = 2 is higher than that obtained at pH = 1. This result is obtained because the higher solution acidity at pH = 1 results in a faster reaction and a smaller curve slope. Likewise, the solution acidity at pH = 2 is smaller, the reaction rate is lower, and the slope is larger. The quantity of sulfuric acid in the solution with pH = 2 is only 1/10th that in the solution with pH = 1. After a comprehensive consideration of the results, the pH = 2 condition was selected to perform subsequent experiments.

In addition to the pH, the digestion temperature also strongly affected the digestion results. Fig. S3b shows the absorbance kinetic curves (525 nm) obtained during digestion of the water sample at different temperatures using the circulating flow method, where the temperature of the thermostats was set to 25, 50, 70, 80, 90, and 100 ℃. During the first 10 min, there was almost no change in the absorbance because the activation energy of the reaction was not provided. After 10 min, the absorbance values began to decrease to varying degrees, and the digestion rate increased with the reaction temperature. The reaction temperature of 100 °C (solution temperature 89 °C, Fig. S2) was selected to perform subsequent experiments.

Next, the digestion time was optimized over the range of 0–30 min (Fig. S3c). For reaction times up to 20 min, the reaction between KMnO4 and the organic compound in water continued with increasing digestion time, and the slope of the ΔA-COD curve increased continuously. For reaction times exceeding 20 min, KMnO4 was sufficiently consumed that the curve slope decreased. Consequently, the optimal pretreatment time was chosen to be 20 min.

Finally, the initial concentration of KMnO4 (10–30 mg/L) was optimized by the single factormethod, and the results are shown in Fig. S3d. The slope of the ΔA-COD curve gradually increased with the KMnO4 concentration, but the final slopes corresponding to concentrations of 20, 25 and 30 mg/L KMnO4 were almost the same after the reaction. This result was obtained because with increasing KMnO4 concentration, both the initial absorbance and the oxidation rate increased, resulting in similar slopes for the ΔA-COD curve. A concentration of 20 mg/L KMnO4 was finally selected considering environmental protection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conditional optimization for COD determination by the CD-FCS
ce:section.ce:para: In the abovementioned experiments, the conditions for the water sample digestion process were optimized. In the proposed method, determination is performed after the water sample is digested. Thus, the reaction time and detection range were first explored. Standard digestion solutions at different COD concentrations were reacted with the CD-FCS in the circulating system to determine the ΔF-COD linear curve (Fig. S4a). To determine the COD in the 0–40 mg/L range, the linear curve was obtained in 10 min, but the detection limit was only 0.5 mg/L and the accuracy was not sufficiently high.

This insufficient detection accuracy was due to the excessive concentration of KMnO4. Using a blank reagent resulted in the KMnO4 concentration in the digestion solution exceeding the detection capability of the CD-FCS. The detection accuracy was improved by appropriately diluting the digested solution. The fluorescence kinetic curve for reacting the blank digestion solution at different dilution ratios with the CD-FCS was determined and is presented in Fig. S4b. The same quenched fluorescence for the CD-FCS was obtained for dilution ratios of the digestion solution + water of 1 + 0 and 1 + 1, indicating that the KMnO4 concentration in the digested solution was beyond the measurement range of the sensor. For a dilution ratio of the digestion solution + water = 1 + 3, ΔF was halved, and ΔF affected the linear range of detection, which is also unworkable. At a dilution ratio of digestion solution + water = 1 + 2, the reaction sensitivity was improved and a large linear range was retained. Therefore, 1 + 2 is the best dilution ratio for the determination of low COD. The ΔF-COD linear curve (Fig. S4c) shows that a 10-min reaction time can be used to accurately detect COD in the range of 0–10 mg/L with a detection limit of 0.1 mg/L, corresponding to a considerable improvement in the detection accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the Cl- on the COD determination
ce:section.ce:para: As Cl- is the main interfering substance in the determination of COD in water samples [3], anti-Cl--interference ability is an important factor for the efficiency of COD measurement methods. Cl- (0, 10, 100, 1000, 5000 and 10000 mg/L) was added to a sample-blank KMnO4 solution and digested, and KMnO4 was determined by CDs-FCS to observe the change in ΔF.

The results showed that the error in COD determination without the addition of a masking agent was 0.24 %, 1.75 % and 3.99 % for Cl- concentrations of 10, 100 and 1000 mg/L, respectively, corresponding to an error of less than 5 %. At Cl- concentrations of 5000 and 10000 mg/L, the error was 7.04 % and 14.35 %, respectively. Thus, the proposed method is resistant to Cl- interference below 1000 mg/L.

The test method was evaluated using KHP as a test sample under the optimized conditions (Fig. 5a). Two curves with good linearity were obtained in the ranges of 0.2–12 mg/L and 8–40 mg/L KHP: ΔF = -48.32c + 678.25 (r = 0.9996, 0.2–12 mg/L) and ΔF = -16.31c + 792.61 (r = 0.9998, 8–40 mg/L). Eleven tests were repeated using 4 mg/L and 20 mg/L COD standard solutions, and the RSDs obtained were 0.45 % and 0.59 % (Fig. 5b), respectively. The lowest detection limit was calculated to be 0.1 mg/L.

Water samples were obtained from different locations (a well (No. 1), a drinking water faucet (No. 2), the Jiang 'an River (No. 3), a Jiang' an stream (No. 4), a lotus pond (No. 5), a sewage treatment inflow (No. 6), a sewage treatment effluent (No. 7), the Ba River (No. 8), the Shicheng River (No. 9), and the Shicheng reservoir (No. 10)) and the COD was determined using the CD-FCS. Then, an addition recovery experiment and a comparative experiment using the National Standard method [29] were conducted to eliminate the interference mechanism and test the accuracy of the measurement results of the proposed method. The measurement results are shown in Tables 1 and 2. The recovery rate for COD determination of all the water samples is between 97 and 102 %, which proves that the measurement results of the proposed method are reliable. The results of the proposed method are consistent with those of the National Standard method, where the T test (P > 0.05) indicates no significant difference between the values measured using the two methods. All these conclusions demonstrate that the proposed method can be used as a novel COD quantification technique.

In this study, a carbon-dot fluorescence microvolume capillary sensor was successfully prepared by immobilizing CA/urea-CDs on the inner surface of a capillary. A highly sensitive method for COD determination was proposed based on fluorescence quenching by KMnO4 and fluorescence restoration by ASA in a recirculating flow system. This COD determination method has good linearity in the range between 0.2 and 40 mg/L COD and a minimum detection limit of 0.1 mg/L. The method has a relative standard deviation as low as 1.04 %, a recovery rate of 97–102 %, and resistance to the interference of 1000 mg/LCl−. The determination results and recovery rates of real samples demonstrate the reliability of this method. The sensor has the advantages of portability, a low cost and easy storage, while meeting the requirements of reduced reagent consumption and high reproducibility. Moreover, the proposed method is especially suitable for water quality detection in the field, which can solve practical problems, such as the difficulty of carrying large equipment and environmental pollution produced by the detection process.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Rong Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yong-Sheng Li: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Resources. Ya-Xiong Luo: Aided experiment. Xu-Yan Zhang: Preliminary experiment. Rong Wen: Carbon dots synthesis. Xiu-Feng Gao: Supervision, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108445.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of preparation methods on morphology of active manganese dioxide and 2,4-dinitrophenol adsorption performance
